schools.summary: God's answer to Moses' complaint: 'then the LORD said unto Moses, Now shalt thou see what I will do to Pharaoh: for with a strong hand shall he let them go, and with a strong hand shall he drive them out of his land' — God answering Moses' complaint (Exodus 5:22-23) with the assurance that he would now act (the promise of the deliverance about to begin, the comfort to the perplexed servant, the deliverance delayed, not denied, God's answer to the trial of faith); 'with a strong hand' — by God's mighty power (the plagues, the strong hand of God on Pharaoh, Exodus 3:19-20, the deliverance to be wrought by God's almighty hand, Pharaoh forced to let them go); 'with a strong hand shall he drive them out of his land' (Pharaoh, so far from refusing, would be made to drive Israel out, the issue of the plagues, Exodus 12:31-33, the complete reversal, the king who would not let them go now thrusting them out, the providence, God's strong hand overruling the proud king) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, JFB.

themes: Then the LORD said unto Moses, Now shalt thou see what I will do to Pharaoh: for with a strong hand shall he let them go, and with a strong hand shall he drive them out of his land
God's answer to Moses' complaint
the deliverance about to begin by God's mighty hand (Exodus 3:19-20)
Pharaoh forced to drive Israel out (Exodus 12:31-33), the answer to the trial of faith

schools.summary: God's self-revelation as the LORD: 'and God spake unto Moses, and said unto him, I am the LORD' — God prefacing his renewed promise with the great declaration 'I am the LORD' (Jehovah, the covenant Name, the self-existent, eternal, covenant-keeping God, the ground of all that follows, the Name that answers Pharaoh's 'Who is the LORD?', Exodus 5:2, the foundation of the covenant assurance); 'I am the LORD' (Jehovah, Exodus 3:14-15, 'I AM THAT I AM') — the Name of God's self-existence, eternity, and covenant faithfulness (the ground of the promises, vv.6-8, each sealed with 'I am the LORD,' the assurance that the unchanging God would keep his covenant); 'I am the LORD' — the foundation of the whole renewed promise (the faithful, covenant God speaking, the answer to Moses' perplexity, the LORD himself the surety of the deliverance, the Name on which the redemption rests, the eternal, covenant-keeping God) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And God spake unto Moses, and said unto him, I am the LORD
God's self-revelation as the LORD
'I am the LORD' (Jehovah) - the self-existent, covenant-keeping God (Exodus 3:14-15)
the Name that answers Pharaoh's 'Who is the LORD?' (Exodus 5:2), the ground of the redemption

schools.summary: The Name not known to the patriarchs: 'and I appeared unto Abraham, unto Isaac, and unto Jacob, by the name of God Almighty, but by my name JEHOVAH was I not known to them' — God having revealed himself to the patriarchs chiefly as 'God Almighty' (El Shaddai, the God able to fulfill his promises, Genesis 17:1; 28:3; 35:11, the name of the covenant-making God of promise, the God who pledged the promises to the fathers); the difficulty — for the name Jehovah was certainly in use long before (Genesis 4:26; 15:7; 22:14, the patriarchs used it), so 'was I not known' cannot mean they had never heard or used the name (the readings: (a) the dominant — God was not 'known' to the patriarchs by the name Jehovah in its full meaning and experience: that is, they knew the name, but did not know, experience, God as Jehovah in the sense now to be displayed — the covenant-keeping God actually fulfilling his promises, the patriarchs received the promises by faith, but did not see them performed, now God would make himself 'known' as Jehovah, the faithful performer of the covenant, in the redemption from Egypt, the name 'known' by the experience of its fulfillment, vv.6-8; (b) some taking it that the full revelation of the name's import, the self-existent, covenant-fulfilling God, was now first given; (c) a minority reading it as a question, 'by my name Jehovah was I not known to them?', the witnesses agreeing the substance: the patriarchs knew God as El Shaddai, the God of promise, now Israel would know him as Jehovah, the God who performs the promise, the covenant-keeper made known in the act of redemption); 'by my name JEHOVAH was I not known to them' (the name Jehovah, the faithful, covenant-performing God, now to be 'known' by the experience of the deliverance, the promises to the fathers now fulfilled, the patriarchs had the promise, the children would have the performance, the name made known in the act) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke.

debates.question: How was God 'not known' by the name Jehovah to the patriarchs, when the name was already in use (Genesis 4:26; 15:7)?

sides.position: Not that they never heard the name, but that they did not 'know' (experience) God as Jehovah - the covenant-keeping God actually fulfilling his promises; the patriarchs had the promise, Israel would have the performance, the name made known by the experience of the redemption (vv.6-8)
The full revelation of the name's import (the self-existent, covenant-fulfilling God) was now first given

themes: And I appeared unto Abraham, unto Isaac, and unto Jacob, by the name of God Almighty, but by my name JEHOVAH was I not known to them
the Name not known to the patriarchs (the crux of the divine Name)
the patriarchs knew God as El Shaddai, the God of promise (Genesis 17:1)
not that they never heard 'Jehovah' (Genesis 4:26; 15:7), but did not know him as the God who performs the promise - made known by the redemption (vv.6-8)

schools.summary: God's covenant to give Canaan to the fathers: 'and I have also established my covenant with them, to give them the land of Canaan, the land of their pilgrimage, wherein they were strangers' — God recalling the covenant he had established with the patriarchs (Genesis 15:18; 17:8; 26:3; 28:13), to give them the land of Canaan (the covenant of the land, the ground of the deliverance, God bringing Israel to the promised land, in keeping with the oath to the fathers); the land of Canaan (the promised inheritance, Genesis 17:8) — the covenant land (the goal of the deliverance, the oath to the fathers to be fulfilled); 'the land of their pilgrimage, wherein they were strangers' (the patriarchs sojourning in Canaan as strangers, Genesis 23:4; Hebrews 11:9, 13, holding only a burial-place, looking for the inheritance, now God would give the land to their seed, the covenant fulfilled, the pilgrim fathers' hope to be realized in their children) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And I have also established my covenant with them, to give them the land of Canaan, the land of their pilgrimage, wherein they were strangers
God's covenant to give Canaan to the fathers
the covenant of the land, the oath to the patriarchs (Genesis 15:18; 17:8)
the pilgrim fathers' hope (Hebrews 11:9, 13), the inheritance to be given their seed

schools.summary: God hearing the groaning and remembering his covenant: 'and I have also heard the groaning of the children of Israel, whom the Egyptians keep in bondage; and I have remembered my covenant' — God having heard Israel's groaning under the Egyptian bondage (Exodus 2:23-24, the cry of the oppressed reaching God, the compassion of God toward his afflicted people, the ground of the deliverance, God moved by their groaning and his covenant); the bondage (the cruel servitude, Exodus 5) — the affliction God regarded (the oppression that moved his compassion and faithfulness); 'I have remembered my covenant' (God remembering his covenant with the fathers, Exodus 2:24; Genesis 15:13-18, not that he had forgotten, but now acting on it, the covenant the ground of the deliverance, God redeeming for his oath, not Israel's worth, the faithfulness moving to redeem) — Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And I have also heard the groaning of the children of Israel, whom the Egyptians keep in bondage; and I have remembered my covenant
God hearing the groaning and remembering his covenant
God moved by their affliction (Exodus 2:23-24)
the covenant the ground of the deliverance (Genesis 15:13-18)

schools.summary: The sevenfold 'I will' of redemption begun: 'wherefore say unto the children of Israel, I am the LORD, and I will bring you out from under the burdens of the Egyptians, and I will rid you out of their bondage, and I will redeem you with a stretched out arm, and with great judgments' — God, sealing each promise with 'I am the LORD,' making the great series of 'I will' promises of redemption (the sevenfold 'I will' of vv.6-8): I will bring you out, I will rid (deliver), I will redeem, I will take you for a people, I will be your God, I will bring you in, I will give you the land (the covenant promises of the full redemption, the certainty, grounded on 'I am the LORD'); 'I will redeem you with a stretched out arm, and with great judgments': God promising to redeem (the word of buying back, ransoming) Israel by his outstretched arm (his almighty power) and great judgments (the plagues on Egypt, Exodus 7-12; the judgment on the Red Sea, the redemption by power and judgment, a type of the great redemption by Christ, the people bought out of bondage, the three acts of v.6, bring out, rid, redeem, the deliverance from Egypt's bondage by God's mighty hand); the 'I will' promises (each sure, grounded on 'I am the LORD') — the answer to Moses' complaint and the people's despair (the certain pledge of the redemption, the covenant of grace foreshadowed, God redeeming his people, taking them for his own, the sevenfold assurance) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

debates.question: What is the weight of the 'I will' promises of vv.6-8?

sides.position: The sevenfold 'I will' (bring out, rid, redeem, take for a people, be their God, bring in, give the land) - the great series of redemption promises, each grounded on 'I am the LORD,' the certain pledge of the full deliverance; the redemption by God's power and judgments a type of the redemption by Christ

themes: Wherefore say unto the children of Israel, I am the LORD, and I will bring you out... I will rid you... I will redeem you with a stretched out arm, and with great judgments
the sevenfold 'I will' of redemption begun (the crux of the covenant promises)
the great series of redemption promises (bring out, rid, redeem, take, be, bring in, give), each grounded on 'I am the LORD'
the redemption by God's outstretched arm and great judgments (Exodus 7-12), a type of the redemption by Christ

schools.summary: The covenant relation - 'I will be to you a God': 'and I will take you to me for a people, and I will be to you a God: and ye shall know that I am the LORD your God, which bringeth you out from under the burdens of the Egyptians' — the great promise — 'I will take you to me for a people, and I will be to you a God': God promising to take Israel as his own people and to be their God (the covenant formula, the heart of the covenant, repeated through Scripture, Genesis 17:7; Leviticus 26:12; Jeremiah 31:33; 2 Corinthians 6:16; Revelation 21:3, 'they shall be his people, and God himself shall be with them, and be their God,' the gracious relation, God taking a poor, enslaved, unworthy people to be his own — the witnesses marveling: God taking the worst of bondmen to be his peculiar people, of his free grace, not their worth, the covenant of grace, the redeemed taken to be God's people, and God their God — the very essence of the covenant, fulfilled in Christ and the church); 'I will be to you a God' (God giving himself to be Israel's God, the highest blessing, God himself their portion, Psalm 144:15, the covenant relation, all that 'God' means pledged to them, the grace of the covenant, God their God, they his people); 'ye shall know that I am the LORD your God, which bringeth you out from under the burdens of the Egyptians' (Israel would know, by the experience of the deliverance, that the LORD is their God, the name Jehovah known by the redemption, v.3, the knowledge grounded in the act, the deliverance proving God their covenant God, the covenant sealed by the experience of redemption) — Calvin, Wesley, Gill.

debates.question: What does 'I will take you to me for a people, and I will be to you a God' establish?

sides.position: The heart of the covenant (repeated through Scripture, Genesis 17:7; Jeremiah 31:33; Revelation 21:3) - God of his free grace taking a poor, enslaved, unworthy people to be his own and giving himself to be their God; the covenant of grace, fulfilled in Christ and the church, known by the experience of the redemption

themes: And I will take you to me for a people, and I will be to you a God: and ye shall know that I am the LORD your God, which bringeth you out from under the burdens of the Egyptians
the covenant relation - 'I will be to you a God' (the crux of the covenant formula)
the heart of the covenant (Genesis 17:7; Jeremiah 31:33; Revelation 21:3) - God taking the enslaved people for his own by free grace
the covenant of grace fulfilled in Christ and the church, known by the redemption (v.3)

schools.summary: The promise of the land sealed with 'I am the LORD': 'and I will bring you in unto the land, concerning the which I did swear to give it to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob; and I will give it you for an heritage: I am the LORD' — God completing the sevenfold 'I will' with the promise to bring Israel into the land sworn to the fathers (Genesis 15:18; 17:8; 26:3; 28:13), and to give it for an inheritance (the goal of the redemption, the promised land, the oath to the fathers fulfilled); the land given 'for an heritage' (a permanent possession, the inheritance of the covenant people) — the goal of the deliverance (the promised inheritance, the covenant fulfilled, the pilgrim fathers' hope, v.4, realized in their seed); 'I am the LORD' (the sevenfold promise sealed, as begun, v.6, with 'I am the LORD,' the covenant Name the surety of all, the certainty, the faithful, covenant God the guarantee of every promise, the assurance grounded on the Name, the redemption pledged by the LORD himself) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Ellicott, Wesley.

themes: And I will bring you in unto the land, concerning the which I did swear to give it to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob; and I will give it you for an heritage: I am the LORD
the promise of the land sealed with 'I am the LORD'
the inheritance the goal of the redemption, the oath to the fathers (Genesis 15:18; 17:8)
the whole sevenfold pledge sealed by the covenant Name (v.6)

schools.summary: The people not hearkening for anguish and bondage: 'and Moses spake so unto the children of Israel: but they hearkened not unto Moses for anguish of spirit, and for cruel bondage' — Moses delivering God's glorious promises (vv.6-8) to the people, but they not hearkening (would not, or could not, receive them, the sad failure of faith, the people, lately believing, Exodus 4:31, now unable to believe, the contrast, the rich promises met with unbelief); the cause — 'for anguish of spirit (Hebrew, shortness of spirit, impatience), and for cruel bondage': the people not hearkening because of their anguish of spirit (their crushed, impatient, despairing state) and their cruel bondage (the harder servitude, Exodus 5, the witnesses noting the cause with sympathy: not mere willful unbelief, but the crushing of their spirit under the affliction, the soul so broken by suffering that it could not rise to faith and hope, the deadening effect of long, cruel oppression, despair choking the promise, yet also a measure of unbelief and impatience, the weakness of faith under trial, the people not laying hold of God's word, the lesson — affliction may so crush the spirit that even God's promises are not received, the danger of despair under prolonged suffering, and the need of grace to believe when the heart is broken); 'and for cruel bondage' (the hard servitude, Exodus 5:6-19, that crushed their spirit, the affliction that choked their faith, the providence, the people too broken to believe, that the deliverance might be wholly of God, not of their faith or works, the trial that prepared them to be saved by grace alone) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

debates.question: Why did the people not hearken to the glorious promises?

sides.position: Not mere willful unbelief, but the crushing of their spirit under the cruel affliction - despair choking the promise, the soul too broken by suffering to rise to faith; yet a measure of the weakness of faith under trial; the lesson that affliction may so crush the spirit that even God's promises are not received

themes: And Moses spake so unto the children of Israel: but they hearkened not unto Moses for anguish of spirit, and for cruel bondage
the people not hearkening for anguish and bondage (the crux of the crushed spirit)
not mere willful unbelief, but the spirit crushed under cruel affliction (despair choking the promise)
the lesson - affliction may so crush the spirit that even God's promises are not received; the deliverance wholly of God

schools.summary: The LORD renewing his charge to Moses: 'and the LORD spake unto Moses, saying' — the LORD, the people having not hearkened (v.9), renewing his charge to Moses (to go again to Pharaoh, v.11, the persistence of God's purpose, not deterred by the people's despair, the renewed commission, the providence pressing on toward the deliverance); the LORD speaking again (renewing the charge after the people's failure) — God's purpose unmoved (the persistence of the divine commission); the LORD renewing the charge (despite the people's unbelief and the coming objections) — God's deliverance not hindered by man's weakness (the renewed commission to go to Pharaoh, v.11, the providence pressing on) — Keil and Delitzsch, Henry, Gill.

themes: And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying
the LORD renewing his charge to Moses
God's purpose unmoved by the people's despair (v.9)
the renewed commission, the deliverance pressing on (v.11)

schools.summary: The renewed charge to demand Israel's release: 'go in, speak unto Pharaoh king of Egypt, that he let the children of Israel go out of his land' — the LORD charging Moses to go again to Pharaoh and demand Israel's release (the renewed demand, as Exodus 5:1, the persistence of God's purpose, the demand pressed despite Pharaoh's refusal and the people's despair, the commission renewed); the renewed embassy to Pharaoh (the demand pressed again) — the persistence (the word of God to the king); 'that he let the children of Israel go out of his land' (the renewed demand for Israel's release, Exodus 5:1, the deliverance pressed, the providence, the demand that would lead, through the plagues, to the Exodus) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley.

themes: Go in, speak unto Pharaoh king of Egypt, that he let the children of Israel go out of his land
the renewed charge to demand Israel's release
the demand pressed again despite refusal and despair (Exodus 5:1)
the deliverance pressed toward the Exodus

schools.summary: Moses' objection of his uncircumcised lips: 'and Moses spake before the LORD, saying, Behold, the children of Israel have not hearkened unto me; how then shall Pharaoh hear me, who am of uncircumcised lips?' — Moses objecting: if his own people, with the glorious promises, would not hearken (v.9), how should Pharaoh, the proud enemy, hear him? (the argument from the lesser to the greater, the discouragement of Moses, his faith weak after the failure, the objection raised); 'uncircumcised lips' (a Hebrew idiom for faltering, ineloquent, or impure speech, Moses' sense of his unfitness to speak, as Exodus 4:10, 'slow of speech') — Moses' diffidence (pleading his lack of eloquence, the weakness of faith, the objection from his own inadequacy, the discouraged servant); Moses' objection (from the people's unbelief and his own poor speech) — the weakness of faith under discouragement (the servant overwhelmed by the difficulty, yet God would answer, giving Aaron as his mouth, Exodus 4:14-16; 7:1-2, the human frailty overruled by God's power, the deliverance not hindered by Moses' weakness) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley, Clarke.

themes: And Moses spake before the LORD, saying, Behold, the children of Israel have not hearkened unto me; how then shall Pharaoh hear me, who am of uncircumcised lips?
Moses' objection of his uncircumcised lips
the discouragement of Moses, the weakness of faith after the failure (v.9)
'uncircumcised lips' - his sense of unfitness (Exodus 4:10), overruled by God (Exodus 7:1-2)

schools.summary: The LORD's renewed charge to Moses and Aaron: 'and the LORD spake unto Moses and unto Aaron, and gave them a charge unto the children of Israel, and unto Pharaoh king of Egypt, to bring the children of Israel out of the land of Egypt' — the LORD, despite Moses' objection (v.12), renewing and confirming the charge to both Moses and Aaron, commissioning them to the people and to Pharaoh (the persistence of God's purpose, the commission renewed, to bring Israel out, the deliverance pressed); the charge (to both the people and Pharaoh) — the renewed commission (overruling Moses' objection, the embassy to Israel and to Egypt, the deliverance pressed by God's authority); 'to bring the children of Israel out of the land of Egypt' (the purpose of the charge, the deliverance, the Exodus, the providence, God's unmoved purpose to redeem, despite the people's despair and Moses' weakness, the commission to the great work) — JFB, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And the LORD spake unto Moses and unto Aaron, and gave them a charge unto the children of Israel, and unto Pharaoh king of Egypt, to bring the children of Israel out of the land of Egypt
the LORD's renewed charge to Moses and Aaron
the commission renewed despite Moses' objection (v.12)
God's unmoved purpose to bring Israel out of Egypt

schools.summary: The genealogy of the tribes beginning with Reuben: 'these be the heads of their fathers' houses: The sons of Reuben the firstborn of Israel; Hanoch, and Pallu, Hezron, and Carmi: these be the families of Reuben' — the inspired record inserting a genealogy (vv.14-27), beginning with Reuben (the firstborn), Simeon, and Levi, to establish the descent of Moses and Aaron (the deliverers, v.26-27, the genealogy of the tribes, the heads of the fathers' houses); the genealogy beginning with Reuben (the firstborn, Genesis 46:8-9) — the order of the tribes (the line leading to Levi, vv.16, and Moses and Aaron, v.20, the descent established); the genealogy (vv.14-27) — inserted to establish the lineage of Moses and Aaron (the deliverers, that their authority and descent, of the tribe of Levi, might be sure, the providence, the deliverers' line recorded, the record of the covenant family) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Calvin, Henry.

themes: These be the heads of their fathers' houses: The sons of Reuben the firstborn of Israel; Hanoch, and Pallu, Hezron, and Carmi: these be the families of Reuben
the genealogy of the tribes beginning with Reuben
the record (vv.14-27) to establish the descent of Moses and Aaron (Genesis 46:8-9)
the deliverers' line traced to the tribe of Levi (vv.20, 26-27)

schools.summary: The sons of Simeon in the genealogy: 'and the sons of Simeon; Jemuel, and Jamin, and Ohad, and Jachin, and Zohar, and Shaul the son of a Canaanitish woman: these are the families of Simeon' — the six sons of Simeon named (as Genesis 46:10), with the note of Shaul, son of a Canaanite woman (the second tribe in the genealogy, vv.14-27, the line leading toward Levi, v.16, and the deliverers, the record continuing); Simeon's sons (Genesis 46:10) — the families of the tribe (the genealogy); 'Shaul the son of a Canaanitish woman' (the note, as Genesis 46:10, of Shaul's Canaanite mother, the faithful genealogy, recording even this, the second tribe enumerated, before Levi, v.16, the line of the deliverers) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the sons of Simeon; Jemuel, and Jamin, and Ohad, and Jachin, and Zohar, and Shaul the son of a Canaanitish woman: these are the families of Simeon
the sons of Simeon in the genealogy
the six sons, with Shaul the Canaanite's son (Genesis 46:10)
the second tribe, before Levi (v.16) and the deliverers

schools.summary: The sons of Levi, the deliverers' tribe: 'and these are the names of the sons of Levi according to their generations; Gershon, and Kohath, and Merari: and the years of the life of Levi were an hundred thirty and seven years' — the genealogy coming to Levi (the tribe of Moses and Aaron, v.20), naming his three sons (Gershon, Kohath, Merari, the heads of the Levitical families, Numbers 3:17, the priestly tribe, the line of the deliverers traced, Kohath the line of Amram, Moses, and Aaron, vv.18-20); the Levitical line (Levi, Kohath, Amram, Moses and Aaron) — the descent of the deliverers (the priestly tribe, the genealogy focused on Levi); 'the years of the life of Levi were an hundred thirty and seven years' (the age of Levi recorded, 137 years, as Kohath, v.18, and Amram, v.20, the chronology, marking the generations from Levi to Moses, the record of the deliverers' line) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And these are the names of the sons of Levi according to their generations; Gershon, and Kohath, and Merari: and the years of the life of Levi were an hundred thirty and seven years
the sons of Levi, the deliverers' tribe
the priestly tribe, the three Levitical families (Numbers 3:17)
the line of Moses and Aaron traced through Kohath (vv.18-20)

schools.summary: The sons of Gershon: 'the sons of Gershon; Libni, and Shimi, according to their families' — the two sons of Gershon (Levi's eldest son): Libni and Shimi (the heads of the Gershonite families, Numbers 3:18, the Levitical genealogy continuing, the line of the priestly tribe); the two sons of Gershon (the Gershonite families, Numbers 3:18) — the line of Gershon (the genealogy of Levi's eldest); the sons of Gershon (Libni and Shimi) — the Gershonite Levites (Numbers 3:21, the genealogy continuing toward Kohath, v.18, the deliverers' line, the record of the priestly tribe) — Gill.

themes: The sons of Gershon; Libni, and Shimi, according to their families
the sons of Gershon
the Gershonite Levitical families (Numbers 3:18, 21)
the genealogy continuing toward the deliverers' line (v.18)

schools.summary: The sons of Kohath, the deliverers' line: 'and the sons of Kohath; Amram, and Izhar, and Hebron, and Uzziel: and the years of the life of Kohath were an hundred thirty and three years' — the four sons of Kohath (Levi's second son): Amram (the father of Moses and Aaron, v.20), Izhar, Hebron, Uzziel (the Kohathite Levites, Numbers 3:19, the line of the deliverers, through Amram, Kohath's age, 133 years, recorded); the four sons of Kohath (the Kohathite families, Numbers 3:27) — Amram the line of Moses and Aaron (v.20, the deliverers' descent); 'the years of the life of Kohath were an hundred thirty and three years' (Kohath's age, 133, the chronology, Levi 137, Kohath 133, Amram 137, vv.16, 20, the record of the deliverers' line) — Gill.

themes: And the sons of Kohath; Amram, and Izhar, and Hebron, and Uzziel: and the years of the life of Kohath were an hundred thirty and three years
the sons of Kohath, the deliverers' line
Amram the father of Moses and Aaron (Numbers 3:19; v.20)
the chronology of the deliverers' descent (Levi 137, Kohath 133, Amram 137)

schools.summary: The sons of Merari, completing Levi's line: 'and the sons of Merari; Mahali and Mushi: these are the families of Levi according to their generations' — the two sons of Merari (Levi's third son): Mahali and Mushi (the Merarite Levites, Numbers 3:20, the completing of the three Levitical lines, Gershon, Kohath, Merari, the genealogy of Levi finished); the two sons of Merari (the Merarite families, Numbers 3:33) — the line of Merari (the third Levitical division); 'these are the families of Levi' (the three lines, Gershon, Kohath, Merari, completing the Levitical genealogy, the record of the priestly tribe, the deliverers' tribe, the genealogy now narrowing to Amram and Moses and Aaron, v.20) — JFB, Gill.

themes: And the sons of Merari; Mahali and Mushi: these are the families of Levi according to their generations
the sons of Merari, completing Levi's line
the Merarite families, the third Levitical division (Numbers 3:20, 33)
the three Levitical lines complete, narrowing to Amram (v.20)

schools.summary: The parentage and birth of Moses and Aaron: 'and Amram took him Jochebed his father's sister to wife; and she bare him Aaron and Moses: and the years of the life of Amram were an hundred and thirty and seven years' — Amram (of the Kohathite Levites, v.18) marrying Jochebed (his father's sister, i.e., his aunt, a kinswoman of the same Levitical stock, Exodus 2:1, the marriage before the later Mosaic prohibition, Leviticus 18:12), Jochebed bearing Aaron and Moses (and Miriam, Numbers 26:59, the parentage of the deliverers, the godly Levite parents, Hebrews 11:23); Aaron and Moses (the deliverers, Aaron the elder, the priest, Moses the lawgiver and leader) — the sons of Amram and Jochebed (the genealogy establishing their descent, of Levi, through Kohath and Amram); 'the years of the life of Amram were an hundred and thirty and seven years' (Amram's age, 137, as Levi, v.16, the chronology of the deliverers' line, the record establishing Moses and Aaron's Levitical descent, the purpose of the genealogy, vv.14-27) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And Amram took him Jochebed his father's sister to wife; and she bare him Aaron and Moses: and the years of the life of Amram were an hundred and thirty and seven years
the parentage and birth of Moses and Aaron
Amram and Jochebed, the godly Levite parents (Exodus 2:1; Numbers 26:59; Hebrews 11:23)
the descent of the deliverers established (of Levi, through Kohath and Amram)

schools.summary: The sons of Izhar, including Korah: 'and the sons of Izhar; Korah, and Nepheg, and Zichri' — the three sons of Izhar (Amram's brother, v.18): Korah, Nepheg, Zichri (the Kohathite line, Izhar a son of Kohath, the note of Korah, the leader of the later rebellion, Numbers 16, the genealogy continuing); Korah (Izhar's son, the cousin of Moses and Aaron) — the leader of the rebellion against Moses and Aaron (Numbers 16:1-3, the note of the kinsman who would rebel, the genealogy recording him); the sons of Izhar (Korah, Nepheg, Zichri) — the Izharite Levites (the genealogy, recording the line, including the future rebel Korah, the record of the Kohathite families) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: And the sons of Izhar; Korah, and Nepheg, and Zichri
the sons of Izhar, including Korah
the Izharite Levites (a son of Kohath)
Korah, the future rebel against Moses and Aaron (Numbers 16:1-3)

schools.summary: The sons of Uzziel: 'and the sons of Uzziel; Mishael, and Elzaphan, and Zithri' — the three sons of Uzziel (Amram's brother, v.18): Mishael, Elzaphan, Zithri (the Kohathite line, Uzziel a son of Kohath, the cousins of Moses and Aaron, Mishael and Elzaphan carried out the bodies of Nadab and Abihu, Leviticus 10:4, the genealogy continuing); Mishael and Elzaphan (the Levites who would bear out Nadab and Abihu, Leviticus 10:4) — the cousins of Moses and Aaron (the Kohathite line); the sons of Uzziel (Mishael, Elzaphan, Zithri) — the Uzzielite Levites (the genealogy, completing the Kohathite families, the record of the deliverers' kindred) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: And the sons of Uzziel; Mishael, and Elzaphan, and Zithri
the sons of Uzziel
the Uzzielite Levites (a son of Kohath)
the cousins of Moses and Aaron (Mishael and Elzaphan, Leviticus 10:4)

schools.summary: Aaron's marriage and the birth of his sons, the priestly line: 'and Aaron took him Elisheba, daughter of Amminadab, sister of Naashon, to wife; and she bare him Nadab, and Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar' — Aaron marrying Elisheba, of the tribe of Judah (daughter of Amminadab, sister of Nahshon, the prince of Judah, Numbers 1:7; 2:3, the marriage uniting the priestly tribe, Levi, with the royal tribe, Judah, the establishing of the priestly line); Aaron's four sons (Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, Ithamar) — the priestly line (Nadab and Abihu would be slain for offering strange fire, Leviticus 10:1-2; Eleazar and Ithamar continued the priesthood, Numbers 3:4, the sons of the high priest, the establishing of the Aaronic priesthood); Aaron's sons (the priestly line) — the genealogy establishing the Aaronic priesthood (Eleazar the line of the high priests, v.25, the providence, the priesthood through Aaron's sons, the record of the priestly descent) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And Aaron took him Elisheba, daughter of Amminadab, sister of Naashon, to wife; and she bare him Nadab, and Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar
Aaron's marriage and the priestly line
the marriage uniting Levi (priestly) with Judah (royal) (Numbers 1:7; 2:3)
the Aaronic priesthood established (Nadab and Abihu, Leviticus 10:1-2; Eleazar the high-priestly line, v.25)

schools.summary: The sons of Korah, the Korhites: 'and the sons of Korah; Assir, and Elkanah, and Abiasaph: these are the families of the Korhites' — the three sons of Korah (the rebel, v.21): Assir, Elkanah, Abiasaph (the Korhite families, the note, the providence, that Korah's sons did not perish in the rebellion, Numbers 26:11, 'the children of Korah died not,' but continued, the Korhite singers and doorkeepers, the sons of Korah of the Psalm titles, Psalm 42; 44-49; 84-88); the Korhites (the line of Korah's sons, spared, Numbers 26:11) — the families that continued (the providence, mercy to the rebel's sons, who became singers in the house of God); 'these are the families of the Korhites' (the Korhites, the line that survived the rebellion, the providence, God's mercy sparing the sons, who served in the temple, the sons of Korah of the Psalms, the record of the spared line) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And the sons of Korah; Assir, and Elkanah, and Abiasaph: these are the families of the Korhites
the sons of Korah, the Korhites
the mercy sparing the rebel's line - 'the children of Korah died not' (Numbers 26:11)
the sons of Korah who served and sang in God's house (Psalm 42; 84-88 titles)

schools.summary: Eleazar's line and the birth of Phinehas: 'and Eleazar Aaron's son took him one of the daughters of Putiel to wife; and she bare him Phinehas: these are the heads of the fathers of the Levites according to their families' — Eleazar (Aaron's son, v.23) marrying a daughter of Putiel and begetting Phinehas (the high-priestly line, Eleazar succeeding Aaron, Numbers 20:28; Phinehas the zealous priest, Numbers 25:7-13, given the covenant of an everlasting priesthood, the establishing of the line of the high priests); Phinehas (Eleazar's son, the zealous priest, Numbers 25:7-13) — the line of the high priesthood (Aaron, Eleazar, Phinehas, the providence, the priestly succession established); 'these are the heads of the fathers of the Levites' (the conclusion of the genealogy, vv.14-25, the heads of the Levitical families recorded, the descent of Moses, Aaron, and the priesthood established, the purpose of the genealogy fulfilled, vv.26-27, identifying the deliverers) — Ellicott, Gill, JFB.

themes: And Eleazar Aaron's son took him one of the daughters of Putiel to wife; and she bare him Phinehas: these are the heads of the fathers of the Levites according to their families
Eleazar's line and the birth of Phinehas
the high-priestly line (Aaron, Eleazar, Phinehas, the zealous priest, Numbers 25:7-13)
the conclusion of the genealogy, the priestly descent established

schools.summary: The genealogy identifying Aaron and Moses, the deliverers: 'these are that Aaron and Moses, to whom the LORD said, Bring out the children of Israel from the land of Egypt according to their armies' — the genealogy (vv.14-25) concluding by identifying 'that Aaron and Moses' — the very men to whom God gave the charge (v.13) to bring Israel out (the establishing of the deliverers' descent and authority, the purpose of the genealogy, to mark the lineage of the commissioned deliverers); the charge — to bring Israel out 'according to their armies' (by their hosts, in ordered ranks, as the armies of the LORD, Exodus 12:41, 'all the hosts of the LORD went out,' the deliverance of the ordered nation, the commission to the deliverers); 'according to their armies' (Israel brought out as ordered hosts, the armies of the LORD, the marshalled nation, Exodus 12:41; 13:18, the deliverance of the people in their tribes and ranks, the providence, the seventy grown into an army) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: These are that Aaron and Moses, to whom the LORD said, Bring out the children of Israel from the land of Egypt according to their armies
the genealogy identifying Aaron and Moses, the deliverers
the deliverers' descent and authority established (v.13)
Israel brought out as the ordered armies of the LORD (Exodus 12:41; 13:18)

schools.summary: Moses and Aaron the ones who spoke to Pharaoh: 'these are they which spake to Pharaoh king of Egypt, to bring out the children of Israel from Egypt: these are that Moses and Aaron' — the verse confirming that the Moses and Aaron of the genealogy (vv.14-26) are the very men who spoke to Pharaoh (Exodus 5:1) to demand Israel's release (the establishing of their identity and authority, the conclusion of the genealogy, the deliverers identified, ready to resume the narrative, vv.28-30); the confirmation (Moses and Aaron the ones who spoke to Pharaoh, Exodus 5:1) — the deliverers' identity established (the genealogy's purpose fulfilled); 'these are that Moses and Aaron' (the order, Moses named first here, Aaron first in v.26, the two deliverers, the leader and the priest, the conclusion of the genealogy, the resuming of the narrative, vv.28-30, the renewed charge) — Gill.

themes: These are they which spake to Pharaoh king of Egypt, to bring out the children of Israel from Egypt: these are that Moses and Aaron
Moses and Aaron the ones who spoke to Pharaoh
the deliverers' identity and authority established (Exodus 5:1)
the conclusion of the genealogy, the narrative resumed (vv.28-30)

schools.summary: The resuming of the narrative of God's charge: 'and it came to pass on the day when the LORD spake unto Moses in the land of Egypt' — after the genealogy (vv.14-27), the narrative resuming (taking up from v.13), recalling the day the LORD spoke to Moses in Egypt (the resuming of the account, the renewed charge, vv.29-30, the bridge back to the commission); the LORD's speaking (the renewed charge, vv.10-13) — the resuming of the narrative (the word of God to Moses); 'in the land of Egypt' (the place of the commission, Egypt, where the deliverance would be wrought, the resuming of the account, the renewed charge to go to Pharaoh, vv.29-30, the narrative continued) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: And it came to pass on the day when the LORD spake unto Moses in the land of Egypt
the resuming of the narrative of God's charge
the account taken up again after the genealogy (v.13)
the bridge back to the commission (vv.29-30)

schools.summary: The renewed charge sealed with 'I am the LORD': 'that the LORD spake unto Moses, saying, I am the LORD: speak thou unto Pharaoh king of Egypt all that I say unto thee' — the LORD renewing the charge, again declaring 'I am the LORD' (the covenant Name, the ground of the commission, vv.2, 6, 8) and bidding Moses speak to Pharaoh all that God commanded (the renewed commission, the authority of the Name, the deliverance pressed); 'I am the LORD' (the covenant Name again, the ground of the charge) — the authority of the commission (the LORD himself the sender, the assurance grounded on the Name); 'speak thou unto Pharaoh king of Egypt all that I say unto thee' (Moses to deliver God's full message to Pharaoh, the faithful embassy, speaking all God commanded, the renewed commission, the deliverance pressed, despite Moses' objection, v.30) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

themes: That the LORD spake unto Moses, saying, I am the LORD: speak thou unto Pharaoh king of Egypt all that I say unto thee
the renewed charge sealed with 'I am the LORD'
the authority of the commission grounded on the covenant Name (vv.2, 6, 8)
Moses to speak all God commanded to Pharaoh (v.30)

schools.summary: Moses' renewed objection of his uncircumcised lips: 'and Moses said before the LORD, Behold, I am of uncircumcised lips, and how shall Pharaoh hearken unto me?' — Moses repeating his objection (v.12): his 'uncircumcised lips' (his faltering, ineloquent speech, Exodus 4:10, the renewed diffidence, the weakness of faith, the servant still pleading his unfitness, the objection that God would answer, Exodus 7:1-2, giving Aaron as his mouth); Moses' renewed plea (his poor speech, v.12) — the diffidence (the weakness of faith under the great task, the objection repeated); 'how shall Pharaoh hearken unto me?' (Moses' discouragement, the proud Pharaoh unlikely to hear the faltering messenger, the weakness overruled by God, the deliverance not hindered by Moses' frailty, the objection that God would answer, ch. 7, making Moses 'a god to Pharaoh,' Aaron his prophet) — Gill.

themes: And Moses said before the LORD, Behold, I am of uncircumcised lips, and how shall Pharaoh hearken unto me?
Moses' renewed objection of his uncircumcised lips
the renewed diffidence and weakness of faith (v.12; Exodus 4:10)
overruled by God's power, soon answered (Exodus 7:1-2)
